The most common use case: a
as direct children. Inserting a wrapper
between them produces invalid HTML. A keyed Fragment groups them without adding a DOM node:
The rendered HTML is a flat
elements — exactly as the HTML spec requires. React tracks each pair by
A similar case arises when rendering table rows where each data item produces two rows (e.g. a data row and an expandable detail row):
Without keyed fragments, the only alternative would be to place the detail
directly in the array alongside the data row. But then
would need to go on each individual
, and the two rows that belong together would have no shared anchor for React.
Keyed fragments for adjacent JSX returns
A component can also return multiple top-level elements by returning a Fragment — when that component is rendered inside a list, the parent assigns the key:
component element (the outermost thing returned by the
callback), not on the Fragment inside it. The Fragment inside is just the component's return value — it doesn't need a key there.
Fragment does not affect the DOM
This is worth repeating because it is the entire point of Fragment: it renders to
. Keyed fragments are purely a React reconciliation hint. The key is stored in React's virtual tree and is never serialised to HTML:

// JSX
<dl>
  <Fragment key="1">
    <dt>JSX</dt>
    <dd>JavaScript XML</dd>
  </Fragment>
  <Fragment key="2">
    <dt>Props</dt>
    <dd>Component inputs</dd>
  </Fragment>
</dl>

// Rendered HTML — no trace of Fragment
<dl>
  <dt>JSX</dt>
  <dd>JavaScript XML</dd>
  <dt>Props</dt>
  <dd>Component inputs</dd>
</dl>
are identical. Use whichever the codebase's import convention prefers:
Keyed fragments for CSS Grid and Flexbox siblings
CSS Grid and Flexbox lay out
. If you wrap a group of grid items in a
, they become one grid cell rather than multiple. Use a keyed Fragment to keep them as direct children:
as props. Any other prop results in a type error, which prevents accidentally treating Fragment like a regular element:
Returning an array of elements as an alternative
Before Fragments were introduced, components returned arrays of elements. You can still do this — each element in the array requires a
. Fragment is generally preferred because it reads more naturally as JSX, but the array form works:
The Fragment form is preferred because it requires only one key per group, avoids the compound-key naming (
), and reads as nested JSX rather than an array literal.

Fragment only accepts key and children
Test the rendered DOM structure, not the Fragment itself. Fragments are invisible to the DOM, so assert that the expected elements are direct children of the parent:
Fragment in context: HTML validity requirements
Keyed fragments matter most where HTML validity forbids wrapper elements. Here are the structural rules that make fragments necessary:
Table layout; browser removes invalid elements
List counter and screen-reader list semantics
Wrapping groups items into one track/cell
You can combine keyed fragments with conditional rendering. The
on the Fragment identifies the group across re-renders even when some children are conditionally absent:
changes from 0 to a positive value, React adds the discount row inside the existing fragment — it does not recreate the main data row.
Mixing keyed fragments with regular list items
You can mix fragments and regular elements in the same rendered array. Each item (whether a Fragment or an element) needs its own
. Keys only need to be unique among siblings in the same array, regardless of the element type:
Fragment has no runtime overhead compared to a wrapper element — it is lighter because React does not create a DOM node for it. For a list of 500 items, using Fragment instead of a

wrapper means 500 fewer DOM nodes, less memory, and faster layout calculations. In practice this difference is noticeable only in very large lists, but it is always strictly better to avoid unnecessary DOM nodes.
Fragments can be nested. Only the outermost Fragment in a list needs a
. Inner fragments (for grouping children inside the list item's component) can use the shorthand
In React Server Components, fragments behave identically to client components — they are transparent to the DOM and accept
for reconciliation when the server re-streams updated UI. The key is serialised as part of the RSC wire format, not as an HTML attribute, so the same rules apply:
When writing stories in Storybook for a glossary or definition-list component, the rendered output in the Canvas correctly shows the flat
structure. The React DevTools panel in Storybook shows the Fragment nodes in the component tree, labelled as
with the key visible alongside. This makes it straightforward to confirm that keys are applied at the correct level.
) when a list item must render multiple sibling elements without a DOM wrapper.
Fragment renders nothing to the DOM — it only groups siblings in React's virtual tree.
